definition: AI targets middle- and upper-middle-wage cognitive work, unlike all prior high-substitution episodes (power looms, containerization, agricultural mechanization) which displaced low-wage manual labor. Historical adjustment mechanisms (geographic migration, upskilling from manual to cognitive) may not apply within the cognitive labor market. Displaced knowledge workers are more politically organized and the segmented labor market complicates the Phillips curve.
evidence: All prior high-substitution episodes displaced low-wage manual labor
Historical adjustment mechanisms may not apply within cognitive labor market
Augmentation-to-substitution ratio (1.0-1.3:1) places AI in historically ambiguous zone
Segmented labor market (white-collar loosening / blue-collar tightening) identified
Historical base rate for technology causing permanent mass unemployment: 0% across all analogues
Base rate for severe transitional disruption in specific occupations/regions: 100%
AI primarily displaces cognitive-routine/white-collar tasks rather than manual labor
Segmented labor market framework: white-collar excess / blue-collar shortage
open_questions: Will the augmentation-to-substitution ratio shift toward substitution as agent capabilities mature?
Do displaced knowledge workers' political organization alter the policy response timeline?
Is the Phillips curve structurally broken by the two-speed labor market?
What is the magnitude of the segmented labor market impact?
Do white-collar workers have better or worse adjustment mechanisms than historical displaced manual labor?
Does white-collar displacement create different macro transmission channels than blue-collar displacement?
